user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: The plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as an advance for a business transaction that failed due to the defendant's default. The suit alleges total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act and claims a refund with interest and costs.

slots.value: Recovery of money with interest and costs

assumptions: Jurisdiction lies with the specified District Court based on cause of action or defendant residence
Suit is within the limitation period

valuation for purposes of court fees in a suit for accounts lies in the discretion of the plaintiff. Therefore, in a suit

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

examples.bad: The cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Court.
examples.good: The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. Further, the advance amount was transferred from the Plaintiff's account at {{BANK_NAME}}, {{BANK_BRANCH}} situated within the local limits of this Court's jurisdiction.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

examples.good: Despite repeated demands, the Defendant neither performed his obligations under the agreement nor returned the advance amount of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Plaintiff. The Defendant's last communication was on {{DATE}}, wherein he sought further time but made no payment whatsoever.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

examples.bad: Under Section 65, the Defendant is bound to restore the amount.
examples.good: Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 provides that when an agreement is discovered to be void, or when a contract becomes void, any person who has received any advantage under such agreement or contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom he received it. In the present case, the business transaction between the parties has resulted in a total failure of consideration inasmuch as the Defendant received Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- but provided no benefit whatsoever to the Plaintiff.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

examples.bad: Cause of action arose on X date.
examples.good: The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform his obligations under the business transaction despite being bound to do so, and the Plaintiff's right to claim refund of the advance amount thereby accrued. The cause of action further and continuously arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice demanding refund, failed, neglected, and refused to repay the said amount.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff files this suit for recovery before the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, State of {{STATE}}, in respect of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as an advance for a business transaction which failed due to the Defendant's default and total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act. The territorial jurisdiction of this Court is grounded in the Defendant's residence and place of business at {{DEFENDANT_RESIDENCE}}, which lies within the local limits of this Court. The Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} at {{PAYMENT_LOCATION}} in furtherance of the agreement, and the agreement governing the transaction was executed at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}} on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. The cause of action arose at {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_LOCATION}} on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and continues to the present as the Defendant has defaulted in repayment and performance, depriving the Plaintiff of the consideration and necessitating recovery from this Court. This establishes a specific territorial link among (i) the Defendant’s residence/business within the Court’s jurisdiction, (ii) the location and timing of the payment, (iii) the place where the agreement was executed, and (iv) the locus of the cause of action, thereby conferring territorial jurisdiction to this Court.

Pecuniary jurisdiction: The suit seeks a monetary recovery of Rs 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of the District Court for money recovery suits in this State, and is properly within this Court’s pecuniary jurisdiction.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} and the Defendant {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} came to know each other in the course of pursuing a business opportunity and thereafter engaged in discussions that led to a formal written understanding, the terms of which are set out in Annexure A (Agreement/Contract). The parties’ relationship originated from negotiations relating to a proposed transaction, and the written agreement was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} to memorialize their understanding.

2. Pursuant to the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff agreed to advance Rs. 20,00,000 (Rupees twenty lakh only) to the Defendant to facilitate the contemplated business transaction. The terms of the Agreement provided that the funds would be utilized for the agreed purpose and that the Defendant would perform the promised acts by the time specified therein and, in the event the transaction did not proceed, would either apply the funds to the stated purpose or return them, all as detailed in Annexure A.

3. By the terms of the Agreement, the Defendant undertook to perform the acts necessary to advance and complete the contemplated transaction and to ensure that the funds were used in accordance with the agreed purposes. The timeline for performance was fixed in the Agreement as {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, with the Plaintiff’s obligation to furnish the funds on the date specified in Annexure A.

4. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff transferred Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant in a single instalment to facilitate the contemplated transaction. The payment is evidenced by Annexure B (bank statement/receipt/UTR), which reflects the date and amount of the transfer and the credited recipient name.

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the agreed obligations by the stipulated performance date and did not repay or apply the Rs. 20,00,000 as contemplated. No portion of the advance was returned to the Plaintiff, and the Defendant did not proceed with or complete the contemplated transaction as per the terms of Annexure A, despite having received the funds and being obligated to perform by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Plaintiff thereby faced a non-performance of the agreed consideration.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff engaged in communications to resolve the matter and issued a legal notice on {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding repayment of Rs. 20,00,000 within the period specified in the notice. Annexure C contains the copy of the legal notice sent to the Defendant. The Defendant replied to the notice on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, the response being annexed as Annexure D. The reply did not rectify the breach or admit liability. Consequently, the Plaintiff filed this suit before the District Court on {{FILING_DATE}} seeking recovery of the advance amount together with interest and costs. The accrual date of the cause of action is {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, and the cause of action is of continuing nature until the relief claimed is granted. filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a restitutionary obligation where a contract has failed for want of consideration, such that a person who has received any advantage under an agreement or contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom it was received. In the present suit, Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, on {{DATE_OF_PAYMENT}} at {{PLACE_OF_PAYMENT}} paid Rs. 20,00,000 to Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, as an advance for a business transaction described in Annexure A. The Defendant defaulted by failing to perform its obligations under the contract, resulting in a total failure of the consideration for the agreement. Therefore, under Section 65, the Plaintiff is entitled to a refund of the entire amount of Rs. 20,00,000 along with such interest as the Court may award and costs of the suit, to place the Plaintiff back in the position held before the contract was entered into. The remedy of restitution is not defeated by any conjectured partial performance by the Plaintiff; the facts demonstrate a complete failure of the agreed consideration because the Defendant did not proceed with the transaction or provide the promised performance, and no value remained owing to the Plaintiff from the Defendant.

In support of the above, the present cause of action is grounded on the legal premise that the money paid by the Plaintiff was received under a contract that has failed due to the Defendant’s default, triggering the obligation of restoration. The Plaintiff further pleads that the amount sought is the principal sum of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest at the rate permissible by law from the date of accrual until realisation, and costs of the suit, with verification to follow in the prescribed form. The Plaintiff maintains that there is no valid defense under the facts and no contractual clause that bars restitution, no set-off or counter-claim and no forfeiture clause that would bar recovery, and no counter-claim exists in the Defendant’s pleadings. The action is valued at Rs. 20,00,000 for purposes of Court Fees and jurisdiction, and the Court is respectfully requested to pass a decree for restitution of the amount along with interest and costs.

Limitation being a matter of procedural rule, Article 47 of the Limitation Act provides three years for suits for money paid on an existing consideration which afterwards fails, with accrual from the date of failure. In the present case, the accrual date is {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}}, and the suit has been filed within the period of limitation on {{DATE_OF_FILING}}; thus, the action is timely. The continuing nature of the cause of action is acknowledged in that the amount remains owing and recoverable by the Plaintiff until actual refund is made, and the relief sought is a monetary decree for the principal amount of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest and costs. Verification of the facts will be given in the Verification Clause herein, and the Plaintiff will attach Annexure A (Agreement/Contract), Annexure B (Payment Proof), and Annexure C (Legal Notice) to prove the sequence of events, together with Annexure D (Reply to legal notice, if any) and Annexure F (Additional supporting documents) as required.

The Plaintiff anticipates likely defenses such as the assertion that there was no total failure of consideration, that some benefit to Defendant was conferred, that a forfeiture clause exists, or that adjustment/counter-claims are permissible. These anticipated defenses are respectfully neutralized by the undisputed evidence of payment (Annexure B) and the contractual agreement (Annexure A) which show that the Defendant defaulted and the consideration failed in total. There is no valid counter-claim; there is no forfeiture clause relied upon by the Defendant in Annexure A; there was no part performance by the Plaintiff that would justify set-off; and the Plaintiff has not taken any other recourse other than restitution of the paid amount under Section 65. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court finds any aspect of the contract still capable of performance, the Plaintiff seeks restitution of the amount already paid on the ground of total failure of consideration, with interest and costs, and reserves the right to pursue any other remedy available under the law.

The Plaintiff prays for relief in terms of a decree for refund of Rs. 20,00,000 along with interest at the rate allowed by law from the date of payment, and costs of the suit; and the Plaintiff seeks such other reliefs as may be deemed fit and proper by this Court, supported by Annexures A, B, C and D as referenced above. Verification of the contents of this Section will be furnished in the Verification Clause, and the Plaintiff respectfully requests that the Court frame the necessary valuation and court fee as per the applicable schedules.

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text: The cause of action for recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as an advance for a business transaction first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} failed to return the advance and perform its obligations under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} having remitted Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} at {{place_names}} in pursuance of the agreement, the Defendant being bound to perform by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} but defaulting, which amount was given as consideration for performance that never occurred and thus there was total failure of consideration within the meaning of Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, entitling the Plaintiff to rescind the contract and demand refund with interest and costs; the origin is therefore anchored to the default date, the money having been advanced for performance that did not occur and the Defendant being in breach. The cause of action further accrued from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} onward as {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} continues to retain the Rs. 20,00,000 and refuses to return it despite demands, constituting an ongoing breach and a continuing cause of action; the Plaintiff, on {{NOTICE_DATE}}, served a legal notice demanding refund, but {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} did not comply, which reinforces the continuing nature of the action, and the cause remains alive as of {{FILING_DATE}} since the money remains with the Defendant at {{place_names}} and has not been refunded; the suit seeks a refund of the advance with interest and costs, with valuation of Rs. 20,00,000 and the appropriate court fees, and the Plaintiff pleads total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act as the legal basis for recovery. filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on Rs. 2,000,000 from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} at the rate of 12% per annum. The basis for this pre-suit interest is that under the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} the Plaintiff paid Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as an advance for a business transaction, which the Defendant undertook to apply towards performance by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} but, on and from {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant defaulted in repaying or applying the amount, leading to wrongful retention of the Plaintiff’s money. The basis for this post-decree interest is to ensure complete recovery of the decretal amount, reflecting the time value of money and the Plaintiff’s financial position harmed by the Defendant’s default. The rate is justified on the same commercial grounds as above, and the decree may specify this post-decree interest so that realization is secure until actual payment.

filled_sections.heading: PRAYER
filled_sections.text:

The cause of action accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and is a continuing cause of action, arising from the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} under which the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to the Defendant in anticipation of performance of the agreed business transaction, which the Defendant did not perform by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, thereby refusing or failing to repay the money despite notice and demand, and the Plaintiff continues to be deprived of the money and is entitled to refund with interest and costs. The suit is properly valued for purposes of court fee and jurisdiction, and the Plaintiff has paid the requisite court fee accompanying the plaint; the Plaintiff claims interest and costs as provided by law, and prays for a decree in the terms hereunder, along with any other relief the Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

(a) Decree for repayment of Rs. 20,00,000 being the principal amount paid by the Plaintiff on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} for the business transaction, which failed due to the Defendant’s default, entitling the Plaintiff to refund of the said amount with costs and such interest as permissible by law, and directing the Defendant to pay the principal amount within 30 days from the date of decree.

(b) Pre-suit interest on the principal amount at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum for the period from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, aggregating to Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}, with this Court directing the Defendant to pay the pre-suit interest along with the principal amount within the same period.

(c) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on the principal amount at the rate of {{Pendente_Lite_RATE}} per annum from {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} until the date of decree, with the amount to be computed and directed to be paid along with the decree.

(d) Future interest post-decree on the decretal amount at the rate of {{FUTURE_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum from the date of the decree until realization, to be computed and awarded in the decree itself.

(e) Costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} in the sum of Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COST}} and any other costs that may be directed by the Court as may be admissible under law, together with such costs incurred in pursuing this suit.

(f) Any other relief, including a general relief clause, the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case, including orders as to costs and such other relief as may be warranted in law and equity.

The rate is justified on the same commercial grounds as above, and the decree may specify this post-decree interest so that realization is secure until actual payment.


PRAYER

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, respectfully prays for the reliefs following in a suit for recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 (Rupees twenty lakh) paid as an advance for a business transaction which failed due to the Defendant’s default, the totality of which constitutes a total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, and for costs and interest as pleaded hereinafter. The cause of action accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and is a continuing cause of action, arising from the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} under which the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to the Defendant in anticipation of performance of the agreed business transaction, which the Defendant did not perform by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, thereby refusing or failing to repay the money despite notice and demand, and the Plaintiff continues to be deprived of the money and is entitled to refund with interest and costs. The suit is properly valued for purposes of court fee and jurisdiction, and the Plaintiff has paid the requisite court fee accompanying the plaint; the Plaintiff claims interest and costs as provided by law, and prays for a decree in the terms hereunder, along with any other relief the Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

(a) Decree for repayment of Rs. 20,00,000 being the principal amount paid by the Plaintiff on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} for the business transaction, which failed due to the Defendant’s default, entitling the Plaintiff to refund of the said amount with costs and such interest as permissible by law, and directing the Defendant to pay the principal amount within 30 days from the date of decree.

(b) Pre-suit interest on the principal amount at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum for the period from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, aggregating to Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}, with this Court directing the Defendant to pay the pre-suit interest along with the principal amount within the same period.

(c) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on the principal amount at the rate of {{Pendente_Lite_RATE}} per annum from {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} until the date of decree, with the amount to be computed and directed to be paid along with the decree.

(d) Future interest post-decree on the decretal amount at the rate of {{FUTURE_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum from the date of the decree until realization, to be computed and awarded in the decree itself.

(e) Costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} in the sum of Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COST}} and any other costs that may be directed by the Court as may be admissible under law, together with such costs incurred in pursuing this suit.

(f) Any other relief, including a general relief clause, the Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case, including orders as to costs and such other relief as may be warranted in law and equity.

draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

BETWEEN

Plaintiff:
{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}} by profession, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.

...Plaintiff

AND

Defendant:
{{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}} by profession, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.

...Defendant


JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff files this suit for recovery before the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, State of {{STATE}}, for refund of an advance of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant in connection with a proposed business transaction.

2. The territorial jurisdiction of this Court is attracted since (i) the Defendant resides/carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_RESIDENCE}} within the local limits of this Court; (ii) the advance amount was paid/transferred by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} at/through {{PAYMENT_LOCATION}}; and (iii) the agreement/understanding concerning the proposed transaction was executed/confirmed at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}} on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}.

3. Pecuniary jurisdiction: The suit seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakhs only), which is within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE

4. The Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into discussions during {{NEGOTIATION_PERIOD}} regarding {{TRANSACTION_DESCRIPTION}} (the “Proposed Transaction”). The Defendant represented to the Plaintiff that the Defendant would {{DEFENDANT_PROMISED_PERFORMANCE}} within the agreed timeframe.

5. Based on the above representations and mutual understanding, the parties executed/recorded a written agreement/understanding dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}} (the “Agreement”), setting out the purpose of the advance, the Defendant’s obligations, and the timeline for performance. The Agreement is filed herewith as Annexure A.

6. Under the Agreement, the Plaintiff agreed to pay an advance of Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Defendant towards {{PURPOSE_OF_ADVANCE}}, and the Defendant undertook to {{DEFENDANT_OBLIGATIONS}} on or before {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}.

7. In performance of the Plaintiff’s obligation, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} the Plaintiff paid/transferred Rs. 20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakhs only) to the Defendant by {{PAYMENT_METHOD}} at/through {{PAYMENT_LOCATION}}. The payment is evidenced by bank statement/receipt/UTR/transfer proof, filed as Annexure B.

8. Despite receipt of the said advance, the Defendant failed to perform the Defendant’s obligations under the Agreement. In particular, by {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}} (being the date when performance was due and the Proposed Transaction failed), the Defendant did not {{SPECIFIC_NON_PERFORMANCE_DETAILS}}. Consequently, the Proposed Transaction did not proceed and the Plaintiff received no benefit/value whatsoever against the advance amount.

9. As the Proposed Transaction did not proceed and the consideration for which the advance was paid failed in toto, the Plaintiff demanded return of the advance. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} calling upon the Defendant to refund Rs. 20,00,000/- within {{DEMAND_PERIOD_DAYS}} days. The notice is filed as Annexure C. The Defendant sent a reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (filed as Annexure D) but failed and neglected to refund the amount.

10. The Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff’s money. Hence this suit.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

11. The Plaintiff pleads total failure of consideration and restitution under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 (as specifically relied upon by the Plaintiff). Section 65 provides that when an agreement is discovered to be void or when a contract becomes void, any person who has received any advantage under such agreement/contract is bound to restore it, or to make compensation for it, to the person from whom he received it.

12. In the present case, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Defendant as an advance under the Agreement for the Proposed Transaction. The Defendant did not perform the agreed obligations by {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}}, the Proposed Transaction did not proceed, and the Plaintiff received no consideration/benefit against the said advance. In these circumstances, the Plaintiff pleads that the Agreement, insofar as it related to the Proposed Transaction, became void/ineffective upon the failure of the contemplated transaction and total failure of consideration, and the Defendant is bound to restore the advantage (i.e., the entire advance) to the Plaintiff.

13. The Plaintiff is therefore entitled to a decree directing the Defendant to refund Rs. 20,00,000/- along with interest and costs.

14. The Plaintiff also seeks pendente lite and future interest under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.


CAUSE OF ACTION

15. The cause of action first arose on {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}} at {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_LOCATION}} when the Defendant failed to perform the Defendant’s obligations under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and the Proposed Transaction failed, resulting in total failure of consideration despite the Plaintiff having paid the advance of Rs. 20,00,000/- on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}.

16. The cause of action further arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff demanded refund by legal notice and the Defendant failed to comply, and again on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} when the Defendant replied without refunding the amount.

17. The cause of action is continuing from {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}} and continues on each day thereafter, including on the date of filing of the suit i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, as the Defendant continues to retain and withhold the Plaintiff’s money without refund.


LIMITATION

18. The suit is within limitation. The applicable Article from the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963 is Article 47 (“For money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails”), which prescribes a period of three years from the date of the failure. In the present case, the date of failure is {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}}. The suit filed on {{FILING_DATE}} is therefore within the prescribed period.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

19. For the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee, the suit is valued at Rs. 20,00,000/- being the amount sought to be recovered. The court fee payable is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}, to be paid as per the applicable State Court Fees Act and rules.


INTEREST

20. The Plaintiff claims:

(a) Pre-suit interest on Rs. 20,00,000/- from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} at 12% per annum on the ground of wrongful retention of the Plaintiff’s money in a commercial/business transaction and the time value of money;

(b) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC from {{FILING_DATE}} till date of decree at 12% per annum; and

(c) Future interest from the date of decree till realization at 12% per annum.


PRAYER

In view of the above, the Plaintiff respectfully prays that this Hon’ble Court may be pleased to:

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 20,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Lakhs only) being the advance amount paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}};

(b) Award pre-suit interest on the principal amount at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum for the period from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, aggregating to Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}};

(c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on the principal amount at the rate of {{Pendente_Lite_RATE}} per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree;

(d) Award future interest on the decretal amount at the rate of {{FUTURE_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum from the date of decree till realization;

(e) Award costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for issuance of legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} quantified at Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COST}}; and

(f) Grant such other and further reliefs as this Hon’ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.


LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

1. Annexure A: Agreement/Contract/understanding dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} relating to {{TRANSACTION_DESCRIPTION}}.

2. Annexure B: Payment proof (bank statement/receipt/UTR) evidencing transfer/payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}.

3. Annexure C: Legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} issued by the Plaintiff to the Defendant demanding refund.

4. Annexure D: Reply, if any, dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} sent by the Defendant.

5. Annexure F: Additional supporting documents, if any ({{ADDITIONAL_DOCUMENTS_DESCRIPTION}}).


VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to {{FACT_PARAS_END}} are true and correct to my personal knowledge and belief, and the contents of paragraphs {{LEGAL_PARAS_START}} to {{LEGAL_PARAS_END}} are based on legal advice which I believe to be true. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.


{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}
Plaintiff

Through:
{{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
Advocate
Enrollment No.: {{ADVOCATE_ENROLLMENT}}
Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}
Date: {{FILING_DATE}}

blocking_issues.issue: Draft contains filing-inappropriate drafting notes / meta-language (e.g., “verification to follow”, “Proper verification will be provided…”, and a “Note: … subject to finalisation by counsel…”), which must not appear in a filed plaint.
blocking_issues.fix: Delete drafting-note/meta-language and keep a single, complete Verification clause in proper pleading form at the end of the plaint.
blocking_issues.location: (i) FACTS OF THE CASE ¶6; (ii) LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS (multiple sentences referring to verification ‘to follow’); (iii) PRAYER (final ‘Note: … subject to finalisation…’)

blocking_issues.issue: Facts are pleaded in a generic/skeleton manner without sufficient particulars of the alleged ‘business transaction’, obligations, and how/when the transaction failed; several sentences are conclusory (e.g., ‘failed due to default’, ‘total failure of consideration’) without supporting factual detail.
blocking_issues.fix: Rewrite Facts as a chronological narrative stating (with placeholders where necessary): what the transaction was, what exactly Defendant promised/was to deliver/do, what conditions/timelines existed, what specific non-performance occurred by what date, and how the transaction was abandoned/terminated leading to total failure of consideration and non-refund.

blocking_issues.issue: Section 65 Indian Contract Act is described/triggered inaccurately in the Legal Basis (treated as ‘contract failed for want of consideration’ / ‘entitling rescind’), without pleading that the agreement was discovered to be void or became void (the statutory trigger for Section 65) and linking facts to that trigger.
blocking_issues.fix: Revise the Legal Basis to accurately state Section 65’s trigger (agreement discovered void / becomes void) and plead, on facts, that the contemplated transaction did not proceed and the agreement was treated as terminated/void leading to total failure of consideration; retain the user-requested Section 65 citation.
blocking_issues.location: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS (opening paragraph and related sentences)

blocking_issues.issue: Cause-of-action / accrual-date placeholders are inconsistent across sections (e.g., {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}}), creating ambiguity as to the operative accrual date for limitation and pleadings.
blocking_issues.fix: Use one consistent accrual anchor aligned to Limitation Act Article 47 (“date of failure”) and tie it to the pleaded failure/non-performance date (e.g., define {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}} = the date Defendant failed to perform/transaction failed). Update CAUSE OF ACTION and LIMITATION accordingly.
blocking_issues.location: FACTS OF THE CASE ¶6; CAUSE OF ACTION; LIMITATION; PRAYER (cause of action sentence)

blocking_issues.issue: Document reference label mismatch: body refers to Annexure D and Annexure F, but the List of Documents includes only Annexures A–C; numbering also starts from 7, which is inconsistent with the list heading.
blocking_issues.fix: Update the List of Documents to include every annexure referenced in the body (at minimum Annexures A, B, C, D, and F) and renumber the list consistently from 1 onward (or adopt a consistent scheme throughout).
blocking_issues.location: FACTS OF THE CASE ¶6; LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS (annexure references); LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

non_blocking_issues.issue: Legal analysis and procedural/valuation material is mixed into the Facts section, reducing clarity of pleading structure.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Keep Facts limited to chronological events; move limitation/valuation/court-fee/verification mentions to their dedicated sections.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Several defensive assertions are pleaded as absolute statements although they may not be within Plaintiff’s personal knowledge at filing stage (e.g., ‘no counter-claim exists’).
non_blocking_issues.fix: Qualify such assertions as ‘to the best of the Plaintiff’s knowledge and belief’ and/or limit them to matters apparent from the agreement/documentary record.
non_blocking_issues.location: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS (paragraph stating ‘no set-off or counter-claim…’ etc.)

non_blocking_issues.issue: Prayer repeats narrative and contains avoidable verbosity; some internal date anchors differ (pre-suit interest period and pendente lite start date should be consistent with pleaded accrual/default/payment dates).
non_blocking_issues.fix: Streamline prayer into specific sub-prayers with consistent date anchors (principal, pre-suit interest period, pendente lite interest from filing date, future interest from decree date, costs).

unsupported_statements.statement: “The suit seeks a monetary recovery of Rs 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of the District Court for money recovery suits in this State…”
unsupported_statements.reason: Pecuniary jurisdiction limits are State/notification specific and not provided in the input; this needs verification for the chosen District Court.

unsupported_statements.statement: “There is no forfeiture clause relied upon by the Defendant in Annexure A…”
unsupported_statements.reason: Annexure A (agreement) text is not provided; cannot assert absence of a forfeiture clause without reviewing the document (or qualifying as ‘to the best of Plaintiff’s knowledge’).
unsupported_statements.location: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS (defensive pleading paragraph)

unsupported_statements.statement: “no set-off or counter-claim… and no counter-claim exists in the Defendant’s pleadings.”
unsupported_statements.reason: At plaint stage, Defendant’s pleadings are not yet on record; cannot state what does not exist in Defendant’s pleadings without qualification.
unsupported_statements.location: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS (defensive pleading paragraph)

unsupported_statements.statement: “the Plaintiff has paid the requisite court fee accompanying the plaint”
unsupported_statements.reason: Court fee amount is left as a placeholder and payment is not evidenced in the input; statement should be verified or phrased as will be paid/has been paid upon filing.
